message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

I've noticed a terminal oddity: when in macos I double click on a word, it selects contiguous characters matching [A-Za-z0-9] and a few others.  Unicode doesn't get selected, and of course spaces dont; most punctuation also causes boundaries.  What is this rule called?

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Understand macOS double-click word selection rules

content.text:
That selection behavior is governed by **word boundary detection** rules, and on macOS specifically it's controlled by a setting called the **"word break" / "selection" character class** — the set of characters Terminal (and other Cocoa text views) treats as "word characters."

A few names you'll see for this concept:

- **Word boundaries** / **word breaking** — the general concept
- **Unicode Standard Annex #29 (UAX #29): Unicode Text Segmentation** — the formal spec defining default word boundaries based on character properties (Letter, Number, etc.)
- **`WordCharacters` / `selectionWordGranularity`** — the actual macOS/Cocoa knobs. In Terminal.app, Settings → Profiles → Advanced has a **"Select the following characters as if they were words"** field where you can add punctuation like `/.-_` so double-click grabs whole paths or URLs.
- **`select-word-chars`** — equivalent setting in iTerm2 and many other terminals.

The reason Unicode letters often *aren't* selected in Terminal.app despite UAX #29 saying they should be is that Terminal historically uses a more restrictive ASCII-ish class rather than the full Unicode "Alphabetic" property — a known limitation. iTerm2 and Ghostty handle UAX #29 more faithfully.

message.content: Across common xterm implementations, what characters are consistently 'within-word' as opposed to 'breaking'?  Just the ones I mentioned, or are there others?

content.text:
Across common terminals (xterm, Terminal.app, iTerm2, GNOME Terminal/VTE, Konsole, Alacritty, Kitty, Ghostty, WezTerm), the **consistently within-word** ASCII set is narrow:

- **`A–Z`** (uppercase letters)
- **`a–z`** (lowercase letters)
- **`0–9`** (digits)
- **`_`** (underscore)

That's it for the reliable intersection. Everything else varies.

### Commonly-but-not-universally within-word

These are within-word in *most* modern terminals but not all (and often only after explicit config in xterm/Terminal.app):

- **`-`** (hyphen) — within-word in VTE, Konsole, iTerm2 defaults; breaking in stock xterm
- **`.`** (period) — within-word in many defaults to keep filenames/IPs/domains intact; breaking in xterm
- **`/`** (slash) — within-word in iTerm2, Kitty, WezTerm defaults (for paths/URLs); breaking elsewhere
- **`:`** — sometimes included for URLs and `host:port`
- **`~`**, **`@`**, **`+`**, **`?`**, **`=`**, **`&`**, **`%`**, **`#`** — URL-ish characters that newer terminals (Kitty, WezTerm, Ghostty) often include by default to make double-click-a-URL work

### Unicode

Modern VTE/Kitty/WezTerm/Ghostty honor UAX #29 and treat **any Unicode Letter or Number** (categories `L*`, `N*`) as within-word — so accented letters, CJK, etc. select correctly. xterm and Terminal.app historically don't, which is the behavior you noticed.

### The honest summary

If you want a rule that works *everywhere* unconfigured, assume only `[A-Za-z0-9_]` — essentially the regex `\w` in ASCII mode. Anything beyond that is per-terminal configuration, and `-`/`.`/`/` are the three you'll most often want to add manually.
